narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admission system uses a model trained on data from thousands of students like you, looking at factors such as early and later school grades, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPA, family income, and whether you'd study full-time to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you specifically, the model estimated a 73% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially, which is high enough that the school automatically rejected your application to prioritize students predicted to succeed right away. The biggest reasons for this prediction were your Year 1 and Year 3 school grade deciles, both ranking in the lowest 10% of your school—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack performance—which strongly pushed the model toward seeing a higher risk of bar failure. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was close to the average and actually helped a bit by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing success. Other factors like planning to attend full-time, your lower-middle family income bracket, and your LSAT score of 31 also offered small positive influences compared to averages, but they weren't strong enough to outweigh your early academic rankings. Overall, your profile stands out as having weaker high school performance than most applicants, who typically rank higher in their deciles and boast stronger LSATs around 37, making the model cautious about your bar exam odds. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience—many students overcome similar starts—but the system weighs these predictors heavily based on past patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking ways to improve or document stronger recent academic performance, and applying to programs with more holistic reviews that value your unique background as a Black woman. You might also explore prep courses targeted at bar success or schools with support for first-generation or lower-income students. Keep pushing; your story is far from over, and with targeted steps, you can strengthen your profile for the next round. We're rooting for you.
